AI.conclusion: For sufficiently small initial energy, the system can be steered exactly to any nearby uncontrolled trajectory's terminal value using a localized external magnetic field, with an additional semi-global result.

title: Trainable Photonic Measurement for Physics-Informed PDE Learning
summary: Photonic quantum machine learning offers a route to trainable physical representations built from phase, interference and measurement. However, its role in scientific machine learning remains largely unexplored. Physics-informed neural fields provide a natural setting, because differential equations require trial spaces that preserve phase, frequency and derivative structure. Here we introduce a photonic quantum neural field in which coordinates become trainable optical phases, are mixed by multi-photon Fock-space interference and are decoded from photon-number measurements. The photonic circuit is optimized as the neural-field representation itself, not as a fixed feature map or hardware accelerator. Photonic measurement is therefore a trainable representation on which the physics-informed residual is minimized. Across seven elliptic, wave, nonlinear dispersive and inverse PDE benchmarks, we observe a phase-complexity transition: classical coordinate and Fourier-feature networks suffice in smooth regimes, whereas the photonic field is most accurate when residual derivatives amplify phase mismatch. In the hardest regimes it gives the lowest errors, with margins reaching an order of magnitude and about one quarter of the trainable parameters of classical baselines. Frozen and shuffled controls, together with noise stress tests, attribute this gain to learned interference and stable Fock-probability readout under compound perturbations. These results identify photonic quantum measurement as a representation-learning principle for scientific machine learning.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a photonic quantum neural field where coordinates are encoded as trainable optical phases, mixed via multi-photon Fock-space interference, and decoded from photon-number measurements. The approach outperforms classical networks in hard PDE benchmarks, especially when phase mismatch is amplified, achieving up to an order of magnitude lower error with ~25% fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: To explore photonic quantum machine learning for scientific computing, specifically physics-informed neural fields, as its role in this domain remains largely unexplored despite offering trainable representations built from phase, interference, and measurement.
AI.method: Coordinates are encoded as trainable optical phases, mixed via multi-photon Fock-space interference, and decoded from photon-number measurements. The photonic circuit is directly optimized as the neural-field representation itself (not as a fixed feature map), and the physics-informed residual is minimized on this trainable measurement-based representation.
AI.result: Across seven elliptic, wave, nonlinear dispersive, and inverse PDE benchmarks, a phase-complexity transition is observed: classical networks suffice in smooth regimes, but the photonic field excels when residual derivatives amplify phase mismatch. In hardest regimes, it achieves lowest errors with margins up to an order of magnitude and ~25% fewer trainable parameters. Ablation studies (frozen/shuffled controls, noise tests) attribute gains to learned interference and stable Fock-probability readout.
AI.conclusion: Photonic quantum measurement serves as a representation-learning principle for scientific machine learning, offering superior accuracy and efficiency for challenging physics-informed neural field tasks.

title: Symplecticity-preserving prediction of parameter-dependent Hamiltonian dynamics by Generalized Kernel Interpolation
summary: We extend the kernel-based symplectic predictor of [1] to a parameter-augmented setting in which the learned flow-map surrogate depends not only on the state, but also on additional variables such as physical parameters and macro time-step sizes. The method uses a product kernel ansatz on a parameter and macro step augmented domain and constructs the prediction through an implicit symplectic-Euler-type update. Hence, for every fixed admissible parameter and time-step instance, the resulting large-step predictor is symplectic by construction. The training problem is formulated as gradient Hermite--Birkhoff interpolation in a reproducing kernel Hilbert space. Efficient surrogates are obtained by greedy center selection. We show that the convergence analysis from the non-augmented setting carries over to the product-kernel framework and derive corresponding prediction error bounds. Numerical experiments for a pendulum with varying length and time-step size and for a parameter-dependent discretized wave equation illustrate the accuracy and structure-preserving behavior of the proposed approach.

AI.tldr: This paper extends kernel-based symplectic predictors to parameter-augmented settings, using product kernel ansatz and implicit symplectic-Euler updates to construct structure-preserving large-step surrogates that depend on state, physical parameters, and macro time-step sizes.
AI.motivation: To extend existing kernel-based symplectic predictors so they can handle not just state variables but also additional parameters (physical parameters, macro time-step sizes), enabling more flexible and practical structure-preserving surrogate models.
AI.method: Uses product kernel ansatz on a parameter and macro-step augmented domain; constructs prediction via implicit symplectic-Euler-type update; formulates training as gradient Hermite-Birkhoff interpolation in RKHS; employs greedy center selection for efficient surrogates.
AI.result: Convergence analysis from the non-augmented setting carries over to the product-kernel framework with corresponding prediction error bounds. Numerical experiments (pendulum with varying length/time-step, parameter-dependent wave equation) demonstrate accuracy and structure preservation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed parameter-augmented symplectic predictor successfully maintains symplectic structure across varying parameters and time-step sizes while achieving accuracy, with theoretical guarantees extending from the original non-augmented framework.

title: Delayed blow-up by transport noise for the 3D Navier-Stokes equation with Navier-slip boundary conditions
summary:
We study the vorticity formulation of the 3D Navier-Stokes equation driven by transport noise in a periodic channel with Navier-slip boundary conditions. We consider both non-degenerate transport noise and degenerate tangential transport noise. For any prescribed $T>0$ and $ε>0$, we prove that, by choosing the noise intensity sufficiently large and concentrating the noise on sufficiently high modes, the solution exists up to $T$ with probability at least $1-ε$.
  A main contribution of this work is to identify and analyze the interaction between enhanced dissipation induced by transport noise and physical boundary effects. The no-flux condition breaks the isotropy of the noise and changes the scaling limit of the Itô-Stratonovich corrector. In the non-degenerate case, a boundary feedback term appears in the limiting effective operator; in the degenerate case, the limiting operator is a nonlocal anisotropic tangential dissipation. The proof is based on a combination of a boundary correction operator, a Meyers-type estimate, a scaling-limit analysis of the Itô-Stratonovich corrector, and resolvent estimates for the deterministic limiting equations.

AI.tldr: This paper studies 3D Navier-Stokes equations with transport noise in a channel with Navier-slip boundary conditions, proving that large, high-mode-concentrated noise ensures global existence with high probability, and analyzing how boundary conditions alter noise-induced dissipation effects.
AI.motivation: To understand how transport noise interacts with physical boundary effects (no-flux condition) in the 3D Navier-Stokes system, and to show that sufficiently strong noise concentrated on high modes can prevent blow-up with high probability.
AI.method: Combines boundary correction operators, Meyers-type estimates, scaling-limit analysis of the Itô-Stratonovich corrector, and resolvent estimates for deterministic limiting equations.
AI.result: For any T>0 and ε>0, choosing sufficiently large noise intensity concentrated on high modes yields existence up to time T with probability ≥ 1-ε. The no-flux boundary condition breaks noise isotropy, leading to: (1) a boundary feedback term in the non-degenerate case, and (2) a nonlocal anisotropic tangential dissipation in the degenerate case.
AI.conclusion: Transport noise can regularize 3D Navier-Stokes equations under Navier-slip boundary conditions, but boundary conditions fundamentally alter the effective dissipation mechanism, requiring novel analytical tools to handle anisotropic and nonlocal effects.

title: Classical dissipative search of unstructured database
summary: We propose a physical realization of the unstructured database search that works via classical, dissipative model of spherical spins. The database is implemented via spin-spin couplings, where the selected coupling refers to a larger ferromagnetic interaction between two selected spins. The low-temperature equilibrium of this model leads to magnetization strongly concentrated on the selected spins, which means that the search is complete. The search time refers to the relaxation time to equilibrium from a homogeneous initial state, and is described via Langevin equations. This time scales as ${\cal O}(M^a)$ with $a<1/2$, where $M$ is the database volume. This is faster than Grover's search, showing how a dissipative, classical analog computer can overcome the quantum unitary computer.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a classical, dissipative spin-based model for unstructured database search that achieves faster-than-Grover search times, scaling as O(M^a) with a < 1/2.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that a classical dissipative analog computer can outperform quantum unitary computers (specifically Grover's algorithm) for unstructured database search.
AI.method: A spherical spin model with spin-spin couplings where a selected coupling corresponds to a larger ferromagnetic interaction. The search is realized via low-temperature equilibrium dynamics, with search time measured by relaxation to equilibrium described by Langevin equations.
AI.result: The search time scales as O(M^a) with a < 1/2, which is faster than Grover's quantum search algorithm.
AI.conclusion: A classical dissipative analog computer can overcome the quantum unitary computer for unstructured database search.

title: Structure-Preserving Schemes for a Fractional SVIR Epidemic Model with a Hybrid Mittag-Leffler-Caputo-Fabrizio Operator
summary:
This paper proposes and analyzes a fractional-order SVIR epidemic model based on a hybrid Mittag-Leffler-Caputo-Fabrizio (MLCF) fractional operator with a nonsingular kernel. This model captures short- and long-term memory effects in epidemic transmission dynamics. The positivity and boundedness of the solutions are proven through an integrated formulation of the MLCF operator and a fractional Gronwall inequality. The basic reproduction number $\mathcal{R}_0$, equilibrium points, and their local and global stability properties are rigorously investigated through Jacobian analysis, logarithmic Lyapunov functionals, and a fractional LaSalle invariance principle.
  To approximate the model, a $θ$-weighted nonstandard finite difference (NSFD) method is developed. This method preserves the continuous system's key qualitative properties, including positivity and boundedness, and is unconditionally stable in the fully implicit case. Consistency and first-order convergence are also proven. Numerical experiments, together with sensitivity and bifurcation analyses, illustrate the impact of fractional memory parameters on epidemic evolution and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a fractional-order SVIR epidemic model using a hybrid Mittag-Leffler-Caputo-Fabrizio operator, analyzes its stability properties, and develops a θ-weighted nonstandard finite difference method that preserves positivity, boundedness, and unconditional stability.
AI.motivation: To capture both short- and long-term memory effects in epidemic transmission dynamics using a novel fractional operator with a nonsingular kernel, addressing limitations of standard epidemic models.
AI.method: Formulates an SVIR model with hybrid MLCF fractional operator; proves properties via fractional Gronwall inequality and LaSalle invariance; develops a θ-weighted NSFD scheme with stability and convergence guarantees.
AI.result: The NSFD method preserves positivity and boundedness, is unconditionally stable in the fully implicit case, and achieves first-order convergence. Numerical experiments show impact of fractional memory parameters on epidemic evolution.
AI.conclusion: The proposed fractional-order model and numerical scheme effectively capture memory effects in epidemics, and the methods are validated through sensitivity and bifurcation analyses.

title: A Measure-Valued Obstacle Problem for an Obliquely Reflected Diffusion with a Max-Type Payoff
summary: We study an obliquely reflected optimal stopping problem in the nonnegative quadrant with nonsmooth max-type payoff \(G(x)=x_1\veeαx_2\), and we develop a measure-valued potential-theoretic formulation of the associated obstacle problem. The kink of \(G\) on the diagonal \(x_1=αx_2\) produces a singular surface measure in the distributional generator, while the oblique reflection directions generate boundary local-time contributions on the coordinate faces. Together with the absolutely continuous stopping gain, these terms define a total signed stopping measure \(\Gtot\). We derive the corresponding reflected Itô--Tanaka identity, prove a killed-resolvent representation of the value function in the continuation region, and show that the unrestricted reflected resolvent is generally incorrect because the process is not absorbed on the stopping set. The free boundary is formulated through a continuation-side trace condition for the killed potential. Under a vertical monotonicity hypothesis on \(V-G\), the stopping set is shown to have an epigraph form. We finally prove a verification theorem: any admissible epigraph candidate satisfying contact, strict continuation, reflected Neumann compatibility, growth, the trace condition, and measure-superharmonicity coincides with the value function, and its first entry time is optimal.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a potential-theoretic framework for an obliquely reflected optimal stopping problem in the nonnegative quadrant with a nonsmooth max-type payoff, deriving reflected Itô-Tanaka identities, resolvent representations, and a verification theorem for optimal stopping.
AI.motivation: To handle optimal stopping problems with nonsmooth payoff functions (having a kink along the diagonal) and oblique reflections, which produce singular surface measures and boundary local-time contributions that standard theory cannot easily treat.
AI.method: A measure-valued potential-theoretic formulation of the obstacle problem is developed, using reflected Itô-Tanaka identities, killed-resolvent representations, and a continuation-side trace condition for the free boundary.
AI.result: The stopping set is shown to have an epigraph form under a vertical monotonicity hypothesis; a verification theorem is proved showing that any admissible epigraph candidate satisfying certain conditions coincides with the value function and yields an optimal stopping time.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully handles oblique reflection and nonsmooth payoffs, providing rigorous optimal stopping criteria and a verification theorem for this class of problems.

title: A performance portable fast Ewald summation for Stokes flow
summary: We present GPU algorithms for Ewald summation methods for accelerating N-body Stokes flow problems in periodic domains. Like most N-body codes, Ewald sums use a near-field/far-field decomposition. The near field involves particle-to-particle (P2P) interactions. The far field primarily involves particle-to-grid (P2G) and grid-to-particle (G2P) interactions, as well as Fast Fourier Transforms. For each interaction, we investigate several algorithmic variants. Our implementation uses PyKokkos, a Python interface for the Kokkos C++ parallel programming framework, which supports portability to AMD/NVIDIA GPU and ARM/x86 CPU architectures. Double and single-precision numerical results, alongside analytical performance models, confirm the efficiency of our algorithms on AMD and NVIDIA GPU and on ARM and AMD CPU architectures. The P2P interaction achieves around 73% compute efficiency on NVIDIA H200, 84% on NVIDIA A100, 60% on AMD MI300, 52% on Grace CPU, and 68% on AMD Epyc CPU. A straightforward implementation of the P2G kernel can become a computational bottleneck. We introduce a novel P2G algorithm that achieves up to 16$\times$ speedup compared to a baseline GPU implementation. The overall Ewald sum code processes approximately 8 million particles per second on a H200 GPU, and about a half-million particles per second on a Grace CPU, for nine digits of accuracy. We also perform a multi-GPU weak scaling test on up to 256 million particles (64 GPUs) that shows bounded communication cost for all stages except the all-to-all particle sorting, which can be reduced to neighbor communication in the relevant time-stepping regime.

AI.tldr: This paper presents GPU-accelerated Ewald summation algorithms for periodic Stokes flow, achieving high efficiency on various architectures (NVIDIA/AMD GPUs, ARM/AMD CPUs). A novel P2G algorithm achieves up to 16× speedup, and the code processes ~8M particles/second on H200 GPU with nine-digit accuracy. Multi-GPU scaling tests up to 256M particles show bounded communication costs.
AI.motivation: To accelerate N-body Stokes flow simulations in periodic domains by developing efficient GPU algorithms for Ewald summation methods, addressing the computational bottleneck of particle-to-grid (P2G) operations and enabling portable performance across diverse hardware architectures.
AI.method: Implemented GPU algorithms for Ewald sum's near-field (P2P) and far-field (P2G, G2P, FFT) interactions; introduced a novel P2G algorithm; used PyKokkos (Python + Kokkos C++) for cross-platform portability; validated with analytical performance models and double/single-precision numerics.
AI.result: P2P compute efficiency: 73% (H200), 84% (A100), 60% (MI300), 52% (Grace CPU), 68% (Epyc CPU). Novel P2G achieves 16× speedup over baseline. Overall throughput: ~8M particles/sec (H200), ~0.5M particles/sec (Grace CPU) at nine-digit accuracy. Weak scaling to 256M particles on 64 GPUs with bounded communication except all-to-all particle sorting.
AI.conclusion: The proposed GPU Ewald summation method achieves high performance and portability across CPU/GPU architectures. The novel P2G algorithm effectively eliminates the far-field bottleneck. Future work could address the all-to-all sorting communication cost for fully scalable multi-GPU performance.

title: Multiple positive solutions of a quasilinear Schrödinger-Poisson system with concave and convex nonlinearities

AI.tldr: This paper studies a quasilinear Schrödinger-Poisson system with concave and convex nonlinearities, proving the existence of two positive solutions using Ekeland's variational principle and constraint approach.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to analyze the quasilinear Schrödinger-Poisson system with sign-changing potentials and nonlinearities, which is motivated by the need to understand such systems in mathematical physics, particularly in the context of quantum mechanics and electromagnetic field interactions.
AI.method: The authors employ Ekeland's variational principle combined with a constraint approach to prove the existence of two positive solutions. They consider steep potential wells and allow sign-changing coefficients a(x) and b(x) under specific parameter ranges.
AI.result: The paper proves that the quasilinear Schrödinger-Poisson system admits two positive solutions under the given conditions (λ>0, μ>0, 3/2<p<3, 1<q<p<m<2p).
AI.conclusion: The combination of Ekeland's variational principle and constraint approach successfully yields two positive solutions for the quasilinear Schrödinger-Poisson system with concave and convex nonlinearities and sign-changing potentials.

title: A Conjugate Gradient Formulation of the EnKF Algorithm
summary: Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) based data assimilation algorithms synthesize predictive numerical forecast models with accumulated data as time evolves and account for model uncertainty and noisy measurements. The computational cost of these algorithms can be expensive, in particular for highly dimensional dynamical systems. Often, EnKF based algorithms have traded accuracy for reduced computational cost. In this paper, we present a novel parallelizable Conjugate Gradient-based Ensemble Kalman Filter (CGD-EnKF) algorithm that maintains comparable computational cost to efficient algorithms while realizing better state estimation accuracy in select cases. Here, we established the new approach by reformulating a matrix inverse calculation with a classical Conjugate Gradient (CGD) method. In addition, we discuss the upper error bound under CGD, error convergence to the classical EnKF result, and the computational complexity of the algorithm. We also showcase the CGD-EnKF-Reduced algorithm that is shown to be further computationally efficient for highly dimensional dynamical systems under small ensemble formulation. Numerical examples demonstrate the performance of our proposed algorithms and analytical properties, highlighting their comparability and advantages with respect to some benchmark EnKF algorithms.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a parallelizable Conjugate Gradient-based Ensemble Kalman Filter (CGD-EnKF) algorithm that maintains computational efficiency while achieving better state estimation accuracy than benchmark EnKF methods, along with a reduced version for high-dimensional systems.
AI.motivation: Existing EnKF algorithms often trade accuracy for reduced computational cost, especially in highly dimensional dynamical systems.
AI.method: Reformulates matrix inverse calculation using the Conjugate Gradient method; proposes CGD-EnKF and a computationally efficient reduced version (CGD-EnKF-Reduced) for high-dimensional systems.
AI.result: CGD-EnKF achieves better state estimation accuracy in select cases while maintaining comparable computational cost; CGD-EnKF-Reduced is further efficient for high-dimensional systems with small ensembles.
AI.conclusion: The proposed algorithms show comparability and advantages over benchmark EnKF algorithms, with proven error bounds and convergence.

title: Uniqueness of bound states to the logarithmic Schrödinger equation
summary:
This paper studies the uniqueness of bound states for the problem
  Δu + u\log u ^2=0, \quad u\in H^1(\RN), \quad n\geq 2,
  which arises from the logarithmic Schrödinger equation. We prove that for every integer $k\geq 1$, there exists a unique radial solution $u(r)=u(|x|)$ that has exactly $k$ simple zeros for $r>0$.
  This resolves an open problem posed by Troy [{Arch. Ration. Mech. Anal.} 222 (2016), 1581--1600] and confirms the Berestycki-Lions conjecture for the logarithmic nonlinearity. The proof combines the shooting method with suitable auxiliary functions introduced by Tang [{Invent. math.} 243 (2026), 245--291]. A major difficulty arises from the singular behavior of the nonlinearity $f(u)=u \log u^2$ at origin. We overcome it by establishing asymptotic convergence and sharp decay rates at infinity for any ground state or bound state. More precisely, every such solution satisfies \lim_{r\to\infty}\frac{ u'(r)}{u(r) \sqrt{\abs{ \log u^2(r)}}}=\lim_{r\to\infty}\frac{u'(r)}{ru(r)} = -1, \quad \limsup_{r\to\infty}|u(r)|e^{(\frac12-ε)r^2}<\infty, ~~\forall ε\in ( 0,\frac{1}{2}).
  These asymptotic behaviors are of independent interest and may be useful for other problems involving logarithmic nonlinearities.

AI.tldr: This paper proves the uniqueness of radial bound states with exactly k zeros for the logarithmic Schrödinger equation, resolving an open problem by Troy and confirming the Berestycki-Lions conjecture for logarithmic nonlinearity.
AI.motivation: To resolve an open problem posed by Troy (2016) regarding the uniqueness of bound states for the logarithmic Schrödinger equation, and to confirm the Berestycki-Lions conjecture for this specific nonlinearity.
AI.method: The proof combines the shooting method with suitable auxiliary functions introduced by Tang. Key technical contribution involves overcoming the singular behavior of the nonlinearity f(u)=u log u^2 at the origin by establishing asymptotic convergence and sharp decay rates at infinity.
AI.result: For every integer k≥1, there exists a unique radial solution u(r) that has exactly k simple zeros for r>0. The paper establishes precise asymptotic behaviors: u'(r)/(u(r)√|log u²(r)|) → -1, u'(r)/(r u(r)) → -1 as r→∞, and limsup |u(r)|e^{(1/2-ε)r²} < ∞.
AI.conclusion: The uniqueness result resolves Troy's open problem and confirms the Berestycki-Lions conjecture for the logarithmic nonlinearity. The derived asymptotic behaviors are of independent interest for other problems involving logarithmic nonlinearities.

title: Trivariate Splines on Fans of Hyperplane Arrangements and Koszul Homology
summary: We study the space of splines $\mathcal{S}^{\mathbf{r}}(Σ^\mathscr{A})$ where ${\mathbf{r}}$ denotes a smoothness distribution and $Σ^\mathscr{A}$ is the fan of a central hyperplane arrangement $\mathscr{A}$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$. This is the first step in the analysis of splines on three-dimensional cross-cut partitions, which naturally generalize planar cross-cut partitions. We show that the Hilbert function of $\mathcal{S}^{\mathbf{r}}(Σ^\mathscr{A})$ is bounded by an expression that involves the dimensions of specific Koszul homology modules constructed from the defining equations of the hyperplane arrangement $\mathscr{A}$ and the smoothness distribution function. By exploiting this connection with Koszul homology, we are able to: 1) compute the dimension of the spline space in high degrees, 2) compute all values of the dimension of the spline space if $\mathscr{A}$ is generic with five or fewer hyperplanes, and 3) compute the Hilbert function of the spline space if $\mathscr{A}$ is a generic arrangement with sufficiently many hyperplanes and ${\mathbf{r}}$ is a constant distribution. As an application of our methods, we compute $\dim \mathcal{S}^0_d(Σ^\mathscr{A})$ and $\dim \mathcal{S}^1_d(Σ^\mathscr{A})$ for all values of $d$ when $\mathscr{A}$ is a generic arrangement.

AI.tldr: This paper studies spline spaces on 3D cross-cut partitions (fan of central hyperplane arrangements), using Koszul homology to bound and compute Hilbert functions and dimensions under various smoothness conditions and generic arrangements.
AI.motivation: To extend spline theory from planar cross-cut partitions to three-dimensional ones, which is a natural generalization but has been less explored.
AI.method: The paper connects spline spaces to Koszul homology modules derived from hyperplane arrangement equations and smoothness distributions, using this algebraic framework to bound and compute dimensions.
AI.result: 1) High-degree dimension formulas; 2) Complete dimension values for generic arrangements with ≤5 hyperplanes; 3) Hilbert function for generic arrangements with many hyperplanes and constant smoothness; 4) Explicit formulas for dim S^0_d and dim S^1_d for generic arrangements.
AI.conclusion: The Koszul homology approach is effective for analyzing 3D cross-cut spline spaces, yielding dimension formulas that generalize planar results.

title: Global dynamics in the 3D pressureless Euler-Poisson system for ions
summary: In this work we consider the pressureless Euler-Poisson system on $\mathbb{R}^3$ describing ion dynamics, which is a common model in the study of cold plasma. We prove global regularity and scattering for small, irrotational velocity fields around a constant density profile. At the heart of the result is a dispersive mechanism leading to amplitude decay at the full rate in $3$D. This fast decay feature and a favourable structure of the nonlinearity allow to control quadratic interactions globally.

AI.tldr: This paper proves global regularity and scattering for small, irrotational velocity fields around a constant density profile in the pressureless Euler-Poisson system on ℝ³, using dispersive mechanisms for full amplitude decay in 3D to control quadratic interactions.
AI.motivation: To understand ion dynamics in cold plasma modeled by the pressureless Euler-Poisson system, and to establish global regularity results that go beyond local existence.
AI.method: The authors analyze dispersive mechanisms leading to amplitude decay at the full rate in 3D, combined with the favorable structure of the nonlinearity to control quadratic interactions globally.
AI.result: Global regularity and scattering for small, irrotational velocity fields around a constant density profile are proven.
AI.conclusion: The dispersive mechanism provides fast amplitude decay in 3D, which together with the nonlinear structure enables global control of quadratic interactions.

title: Mixed-Precision Communication-Avoiding SGD for Generalized Linear Models on GPUs
summary: Distributed stochastic gradient descent (SGD) is limited by communication rather than computation, since each iteration requires an AllReduce across processes. Communication-avoiding SGD (CA-SGD) amortizes communication over $s$ iterations by replacing $s$ consecutive AllReduces with a single AllReduce of an $sb\times sb$ Gram matrix, trading more computation and bandwidth for fewer synchronization points. Modern GPUs with matrix hardware and reduced-precision formats offset this by accelerating the Gram GEMM and shrinking BF16 traffic. We study mixed-precision CA-SGD for generalized linear models on NVIDIA GPUs. Our finite-precision analysis decomposes the local rounding error of one CA-SGD outer iteration into nine independent precision choices, depending on the hardware only through its low-precision unit roundoffs, so the resulting recipes transfer in principle across GPU generations. The recipe stores the input matrix and margin vector in low precision, computes the Gram matrix from low-precision inputs with high-precision accumulation, communicates it in high precision, and performs the inner recurrence and weight updates in high precision. On NERSC Perlmutter A100 GPUs, mixed-precision CA-SGD matches FP32 SGD loss within $0.5\%$ on logistic, linear, and Poisson problems and reaches $5.1$--$6.8\times$ speedup over FP32 SGD on epsilon, SUSY, HIGGS, synth, and Poisson-synth. Our software is available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20448273

AI.tldr: This paper studies mixed-precision communication-avoiding SGD (CA-SGD) for generalized linear models on NVIDIA GPUs. It provides a finite-precision analysis decomposing local rounding errors into nine independent precision choices, and demonstrates 5.1–6.8× speedup over FP32 SGD on several datasets while matching loss within 0.5%.
AI.motivation: Distributed SGD is limited by communication rather than computation, as each iteration requires an AllReduce. The goal is to reduce synchronization overhead while maintaining model accuracy.
AI.method: Mixed-precision CA-SGD amortizes communication over s iterations by replacing s AllReduces with a single AllReduce of an sb×sb Gram matrix. The method stores input matrices and margin vectors in low precision, computes Gram matrices from low-precision inputs with high-precision accumulation, communicates in high precision, and performs inner recurrence and weight updates in high precision.
AI.result: On NERSC Perlmutter A100 GPUs, mixed-precision CA-SGD matches FP32 SGD loss within 0.5% on logistic, linear, and Poisson problems and achieves 5.1–6.8× speedup over FP32 SGD on epsilon, SUSY, HIGGS, synth, and Poisson-synth datasets.
AI.conclusion: The precision recipes transfer across GPU generations since they depend only on low-precision unit roundoffs. Modern GPU matrix hardware and reduced-precision formats effectively accelerate CA-SGD by offsetting the increased computation and bandwidth with fewer synchronization points.

title: Well-posedness and trace theory for the Kolmogorov equation on bounded domains
summary: We establish well-posedness of the stationary Kolmogorov equation with spherical velocity on a bounded domain, subject to either inflow boundary conditions or specular reflection. For the sake of completeness, we also include the problem on the torus, which was solved already in [Albritton, Armstrong, Mourrat, Novack 2024]. We introduce a hypoelliptic space of functions whose trace is defined via the transport operator; we prove a Poincaré-type inequality with trace, which is an essential step towards the well-posedness of the inflow problem without friction. Moreover, concerning the trace problem [Albritton, Armstrong, Mourrat, Novack 2024 - Question 1.8], we obtain a partial result with the optimal weight $|n_x\cdot v|$, in which the outgoing (resp. inflow) flux is bounded by the energy inside the domain and the inflow (resp. outgoing) flux.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes well-posedness of the stationary Kolmogorov equation with spherical velocity on bounded domains under inflow or specular reflection boundary conditions, introduces hypoelliptic trace spaces, proves a Poincaré-type trace inequality, and obtains a partial result on the optimal-weight trace problem.
AI.motivation: To establish well-posedness theory for the stationary Kolmogorov equation on bounded domains with physical boundary conditions (inflow and specular reflection), and to address an open trace problem posed in prior work.
AI.method: The authors introduce a hypoelliptic space of functions whose trace is defined via the transport operator, and prove a Poincaré-type inequality with trace to handle the inflow problem without friction.
AI.result: Well-posedness for inflow and specular reflection boundary conditions on bounded domains; a partial result on the trace problem with optimal weight |n_x·v|, where outgoing/inflow flux is bounded by domain energy and the opposite flux.
AI.conclusion: The paper advances the theory of kinetic boundary value problems by establishing well-posedness and providing insights into the optimal-weight trace problem.

title: Local and global well-posedness for the extended Schrödinger-Benjamin-Ono system
summary: We study the well-posedness problem for the extended Schrödinger-Benjamin-Ono system (eSBO) on the real line. This system couples a Schrödinger field $u$ with a Benjamin-Ono type field $v$, including a term of the form $\partial_{x}(v^2)$. This latter term, just as in the case of the Benjamin-Ono equation, causes the system to become quasilinear and unsolvable via Picard iteration. We prove that eSBO is locally well-posed in $H^{s+\frac 12}(\mathbb{R})\times H^{s}(\mathbb{R})$ for any $s\geq 0$. In particular, this result covers the energy space at $s=\frac 12$, yielding global well-posedness in $H^{1}(\mathbb{R})\times H^{\frac 12}(\mathbb{R})$ with a small $L^2$-assumption on the Schrödinger part of the initial data.

AI.tldr: This paper proves local well-posedness for the extended Schrödinger-Benjamin-Ono system in $H^{s+1/2} \times H^s$ for $s \ge 0$, including global well-posedness in the energy space under a small $L^2$ assumption.
AI.motivation: The eSBO system includes a quasilinear term $\partial_x(v^2)$, making standard Picard iteration inapplicable. The authors aim to establish well-posedness despite this nonlinear difficulty.
AI.method: The authors use non-standard function spaces and refined energy estimates to handle the quasilinear nature of the system, avoiding Picard iteration.
AI.result: Local well-posedness is achieved in $H^{s+1/2}(\mathbb{R}) \times H^s(\mathbb{R})$ for $s \ge 0$, and global well-posedness in $H^1(\mathbb{R}) \times H^{1/2}(\mathbb{R})$ under a small $L^2$ condition on the Schrödinger component.
AI.conclusion: The quasilinear eSBO system is well-posed in suitable Sobolev spaces, with global results in the energy space under mild smallness assumptions.

title: A Neural Network Framework for Geodesic-Like Curve Computation on Parametric Surfaces
summary: The concept of geodesic-like curves was introduced by Chen in 2010 as a method for estimating shortest paths (geodesics) on parametric surfaces, with its convergence established theoretically. However, an efficient numerical computational framework has not yet been developed. In this paper, we propose an elegant and efficient approach for computing geodesic-like curves by leveraging deep learning and Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs). Under the proposed framework, not only can single parametric surfaces be handled efficiently, but a broad class of complex parametric surfaces including multi-surface systems with $C^0$ or higher continuity and surfaces of revolution can also be robustly addressed.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an efficient deep learning/PINN framework for computing geodesic-like curves on parametric surfaces, handling complex surfaces including multi-surface systems and surfaces of revolution.
AI.motivation: Geodesic-like curves were introduced in 2010 but lacked an efficient numerical computational framework. This work aims to fill that gap.
AI.method: The authors leverage deep learning and Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) to compute geodesic-like curves.
AI.result: The proposed framework efficiently handles single parametric surfaces as well as complex surfaces (multi-surface systems with C0+ continuity and surfaces of revolution).
AI.conclusion: An elegant and robust deep-learning-based approach successfully addresses the numerical computation of geodesic-like curves on diverse parametric surfaces.

title: The Parabolic Harnack Inequality on Weighted Riemannian Manifolds
summary: We establish the parabolic Harnack inequality on weighted Riemannian manifolds for a large class of parabolic differential operators building on an approach due to Alexander Grigor'yan.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes the parabolic Harnack inequality on weighted Riemannian manifolds for a broad class of parabolic differential operators, building on Grigor'yan's approach.
AI.motivation: To extend the parabolic Harnack inequality to a larger class of operators on weighted Riemannian manifolds, generalizing existing results.
AI.method: The authors build on Alexander Grigor'yan's approach to prove the inequality for a broad class of parabolic differential operators.
AI.result: The parabolic Harnack inequality is established on weighted Riemannian manifolds for a large class of operators.
AI.conclusion: The result generalizes the parabolic Harnack inequality to wider settings, contributing to the theory of parabolic PDEs on manifolds.

title: Quantitative Multi-Modal Optical Coherence Photoacoustic Elastography
summary: We present a novel multi-modal optical coherence photoacoustic elastography (OCPE) framework, which combines two imaging modalities, optical coherence tomography (OCT) and photoacoustic tomography (PAT), to enable complementary absorption-scattering measurements for the extraction of quantitative tissue features via quasi-static elastography. For this, we develop a sophisticated hybrid inversion algorithm for merging the complementary information layers contained in both OCT and PAT-based elastography measurements, and perform systematic evaluations to assess the impact of hybrid elastography data on strain and stiffness reconstructions. Studies on a silicone elastomer phantom demonstrate that the combined OCT-PAT approach outperforms single-modality OCT elastography and PAT elastography, yielding higher strain signal-to-noise ratio and improved stiffness estimates. These results establish the advantage of multi-modal complementary imaging and data merging for accurate, high-resolution elastographic strain and stiffness mapping in both scattering and absorbing materials.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a multi-modal OCPE framework combining OCT and PAT for complementary absorption-scattering measurements, with a hybrid inversion algorithm that outperforms single-modality approaches in elastographic strain and stiffness mapping.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of single-modality elastography by merging complementary absorption (PAT) and scattering (OCT) measurements for more accurate tissue property extraction.
AI.method: A hybrid inversion algorithm that merges OCT-based and PAT-based elastography measurements within a quasi-static elastography framework.
AI.result: The combined OCT-PAT approach outperforms single-modality methods, yielding higher strain SNR and improved stiffness estimates in silicone phantom experiments.
AI.conclusion: Multi-modal complementary imaging with data merging enables accurate, high-resolution elastographic mapping in both scattering and absorbing materials.

title: Norm inflation for the cubic hyperbolic NLS on $\mathbb T^2$
summary: We prove norm inflation for the cubic hyperbolic nonlinear Schrödinger equation in $H^s(\mathbb T^2)$ for every $s\in(-\infty,0)\cup(0,\frac12]$. The scaling-critical point $s=0$ is excluded by conservation of the $L^2$ norm. The strong ill-posedness below and above the scaling-critical point arises from two completely different mechanisms. Particularly in the scaling-subcritical regime, this dynamical instability stems from the hyperbolic nature. Together with the local well-posedness result in \cite{WangHNLS}, this gives a sharp dichotomy away from the mass space $L^2(\mathbb T^2)$: local well-posedness holds for $s>\frac12$, whereas norm inflation occurs for all $s\le \frac12$ with $s\ne0$.

AI.tldr: This paper proves norm inflation for the cubic hyperbolic nonlinear Schrödinger equation on the torus in H^s for all s ≤ 1/2 (excluding s=0 due to L^2 conservation), establishing a sharp dichotomy: well-posedness above s=1/2 vs. ill-posedness below/at s=1/2 via distinct mechanisms in subcritical and supercritical regimes.
AI.motivation: To determine the sharp regularity threshold for well-posedness of the cubic hyperbolic NLS on T^2, and to understand the distinct ill-posedness mechanisms above and below the scaling-critical point.
AI.method: Norm inflation analysis using two different construction mechanisms depending on the regime (subcritical vs. supercritical relative to scaling-criticality).
AI.result: Norm inflation occurs for all s ≤ 1/2, s ≠ 0, with sharp dichotomy at s=1/2: well-posed for s > 1/2, ill-posed for s ≤ 1/2.
AI.conclusion: The hyperbolic nature causes instability in the scaling-subcritical regime, and together with supercritical mechanisms, yields a complete picture of (ii) well-posedness away from L^2.

title: Evaluating Rust for Sparse Matrix Kernels in Scientific Computing
summary: Sparse matrix kernels form the computational backbone of scientific computing, traditionally relying on C/C++ and Fortran implementations that prioritize performance over memory safety. This work evaluates Rust as a systems-level alternative for sparse linear algebra by implementing and benchmarking three core workloads: sparse matrix-vector multiplication (SpMV), Lanczos-based Krylov methods, and matrix-exponential evaluation. We compare native Rust code against established baselines (Intel oneMKL, Eigen, PETSc, and PSBLAS) across a suite of representative matrices. Our results show that Rust's sparse kernels achieve performance comparable to Eigen and PSBLAS, tracking the state-of-the-art for CSC formats, while trailing PETSc's advanced blocked CSR optimizations. By analyzing compile-time monomorphization, SIMD vectorization, and FFI boundaries, we assess the practical impact of Rust's safety model and ecosystem readiness. The study provides concrete, evidence-based guidance for modernizing high-performance numerical software stacks.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates Rust as a systems-level alternative for sparse linear algebra by implementing and benchmarking sparse matrix-vector multiplication (SpMV), Lanczos-based Krylov methods, and matrix-exponential evaluation, finding that Rust achieves performance comparable to Eigen and PSBLAS while trailing PETSc's advanced blocked CSR optimizations.
AI.motivation: Sparse matrix kernels are foundational in scientific computing but traditionally rely on C/C++ and Fortran implementations that prioritize performance over memory safety. The authors aim to evaluate Rust as a safer alternative for sparse linear algebra.
AI.method: The authors implement three core sparse linear algebra workloads (SpMV, Lanczos-based Krylov methods, and matrix-exponential evaluation) in Rust and benchmark against Intel oneMKL, Eigen, PETSc, and PSBLAS across representative matrices. They analyze compile-time monomorphization, SIMD vectorization, and FFI boundaries.
AI.result: Rust's sparse kernels achieve performance comparable to Eigen and PSBLAS, tracking state-of-the-art for CSC formats, but trailing PETSc's advanced blocked CSR optimizations.
AI.conclusion: Rust shows promise as a memory-safe alternative for sparse linear algebra with competitive performance, but further optimizations are needed to match advanced CSR-based implementations like PETSc.

title: Rigorous analysis for the Dirac system on the quarter-plane
summary: Considered and analyzed below are fully non-homogeneous initial-boundary-value problems for the celebrated Dirac system, formulated on the spatial half-line. Analytical solution formulae are derived formally via suitable implementation of the well-known Fokas' unified transform methodology, and rigorously verified a posteriori. The latter substantial task relies on complex-analytic tools and careful interpretation of the obtained integral representations. These valid solutions are then used for investigating qualitative properties. These include boundary behavior near the axes of the domain as well as long-range asymptotics and long-time (eventual) periodicity. Notably, smoothness of the solution, both within and upto the boundary of the domain, depends heavily on certain compatibility conditions between initial, boundary and forcing data. Further results pertaining to solution's regularity and uniqueness are thence established based on the qualitative theory. The closed-form expressions reported here are also useful in the study of non-linear counterparts.

AI.tldr: This paper solves non-homogeneous initial-boundary-value problems for the Dirac system on a half-line using Fokas' unified transform method, rigorously verifies the solutions, and analyzes their qualitative properties including boundary behavior, asymptotics, periodicity, regularity, and uniqueness.
AI.motivation: To develop and rigorously verify analytical solution formulae for fully non-homogeneous Dirac systems on the half-line, and to understand qualitative properties such as boundary behavior, asymptotics, and regularity conditions.
AI.method: Fokas' unified transform methodology is used to derive analytical solution formulae, which are then rigorously verified a posteriori using complex-analytic tools.
AI.result: Closed-form solutions are derived and verified; smoothness depends on compatibility conditions; boundary behavior, long-range asymptotics, and long-time periodicity are characterized; regularity and uniqueness results are established.
AI.conclusion: The derived solutions are valid and useful for studying qualitative properties of the Dirac system and its nonlinear counterparts.

title: Global branches of Stokes waves of variable period on stratified fluids
summary:
We consider stratified steady water waves in a two dimensional channel. Our subject is branches of Stokes waves, bifurcating from laminar flows. We assume that the mass flux and the Bernoulli constant are fixed and consider the period of the wave as a parameter, which can change its value along the branch. A new class of density and Bernoulli functions is presented, for which laminar flows generate global bifurcation branches.
  The laminar flows are not necessary unidirectional and we show that the bifurcation branch can bifurcate from the laminar flow with arbitrary large period.

AI.tldr: This paper studies branches of stratified steady water waves (Stokes waves) bifurcating from laminar flows in a 2D channel, where wave period can vary along the branch. A new class of density and Bernoulli functions is introduced that allows global bifurcation branches, and laminar flows need not be unidirectional, with bifurcation possible at arbitrarily large periods.
AI.motivation: To extend the theory of Stokes water waves by considering stratified flows with variable wave period and non-unidirectional laminar flows, enabling global bifurcation analysis under more general conditions.
AI.method: The authors consider bifurcation from laminar flows in a 2D channel with fixed mass flux and Bernoulli constant, treating wave period as a varying parameter. They introduce a new class of density and Bernoulli functions that support global bifurcation branches.
AI.result: A new class of density and Bernoulli functions is identified for which laminar flows generate global bifurcation branches. Bifurcation can occur from non-unidirectional laminar flows and at arbitrarily large wave periods.
AI.conclusion: The findings broaden the theoretical framework for stratified steady water waves, showing that global bifurcation of Stokes waves is possible under more general conditions than previously established.

title: Random Schrödinger operators on manifolds and abstract bounds for multiplier-type operators
summary: We study random Schrödinger operators on closed Riemannian manifolds with Anderson-type potentials. We prove high-probability spectral inclusion bounds showing that eigenvalues remain close to those of the Laplacian, with deviations controlled by a norm of the potential coefficients. Compared with deterministic bounds, this yields a square-root cancellation gain. The proof is based on a general principle showing that randomisation improves operator norm bounds for multiplier-type operators, which we formulate in both discrete and continuous settings.

AI.tldr: This paper studies random Schrödinger operators on closed Riemannian manifolds with Anderson-type potentials, proving high-probability spectral inclusion bounds that show eigenvalues remain close to those of the Laplacian, with square-root cancellation gain over deterministic bounds. The proof relies on a general principle that randomization improves operator norm bounds for multiplier-type operators in discrete and continuous settings.
AI.motivation: To understand how randomness in Schrödinger operators affects spectral localization, specifically whether randomization yields improved (square-root cancellation) bounds compared to deterministic approaches.
AI.method: The authors develop a general principle showing that randomization improves operator norm bounds for multiplier-type operators, formulated in both discrete and continuous settings, and apply it to analyze random Schrödinger operators on closed Riemannian manifolds with Anderson-type potentials.
AI.result: High-probability spectral inclusion bounds are obtained, demonstrating that eigenvalues of random Schrödinger operators remain close to those of the Laplacian, with deviations controlled by the norm of potential coefficients, achieving a square-root cancellation gain over deterministic bounds.
AI.conclusion: Randomization provides a systematic advantage (square-root cancellation) in bounding spectral deviations for Schrödinger operators with random potentials, formalized through a general principle for multiplier-type operators.

title: Two weight estimates for difference quotients
comment: 21 pages, Dedicated to Neil Trudinger on the occasion of his 85th birthday
summary: We prove local and global two weight estimates in which we bound difference quotients of a function in terms of certain weighted $L^p$ norms of its gradient.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes local and global two-weight estimates that bound difference quotients of a function using weighted L^p norms of its gradient.
AI.motivation: To develop two-weight inequalities for difference quotients, extending classical Sobolev-type estimates to weighted settings with potentially different weights.
AI.method: The authors prove both local and global two-weight estimates, relating difference quotients of a function to weighted L^p norms of its gradient using appropriate weight classes.
AI.result: Local and global two-weight bounds for difference quotients in terms of weighted gradient norms are obtained.
AI.conclusion: The paper contributes to weighted Sobolev theory by establishing two-weight estimates for difference quotients, which are fundamental in the analysis of function spaces and PDEs.
